articles.title: Domain 1 Pathway — Caregiving Adequacy

head: Phase
Action
Owner
rows: Identify primary caregiver + backup; family meeting if needed; document agreement
Caregiver assessment (Q6b asked privately); peer connection to one experienced caregiver
Caregiver basic skills training (bathing, lifting, medication, bed-sore prevention)
Caregiver support group introduction; respite arrangement if burden detected
Re-assess Q6 and Q6b; adjust support if gaps remain
Family meeting to renew task-sharing; rotate caregiving burden if multiple family members available

content: Q6 and Q6b scores at 1 or below by Month 6; primary caregiver identifies as coping.

articles.title: Domain 3 Pathway — Health & Medical

head: Phase
Action
Owner
rows: PHC/UPHC enrolment confirmed; consolidated medication list created with family
First clinical follow-up scheduled; transport arranged if elder cannot travel alone
Medication supply addressed (PHC stockout escalation, Janaushadhi linkage, family budget conversation)
Sensory function check (Q13a); refer to next eye/ENT camp if needed
Re-assess Q11, Q12, Q13a, Q13b; document medication adherence trajectory
Clinical review with PHC; entitlements team verification on disability certificate if applicable

content: ≥80% medication adherence (Q12 ≤1); NCDs under control where applicable; vision/hearing addressed where operable.

articles.title: Domain 4 Pathway — Cognitive & Emotional

head: Phase
Action
Owner
rows: Q15a/b assessed privately; mental health screening explained; consent for referral if positive
Referral made (PHC counsellor / NIMHANS DMHP / NGO partner); centre engagement arranged for isolation
Confirm referral attended; centre attendance for ambulant elders; home-based engagement for non-ambulant
Re-assess Q15a and Q15b; if cognitive concerns (Q14), reversible-cause workup at PHC
Continuing counselling check; peer mentor pairing for Tier 3 stable elders; family conversation about visit patterns
Full re-EVA; mental health trajectory documented

content: Q15a and Q15b trending toward 0–1 over 6 months; reduced isolation through centre or community contact; referrals kept.

articles.title: Domain 5 Pathway — Safety, Abuse & Protection
content: **Safety pathway differs from other domains:** the elder's safety overrides standard care pathway timelines. Steps below are minimum standards; the supervisor and city lead may compress or intensify as the case demands.

head: Phase
Action
Owner
rows: Critical flag noted; supervisor informed same day; restricted log entry created; CO does not act alone
Risk assessment by Sup + City Lead; safety plan drafted; helplines briefed if needed
Safety plan implemented; trusted contact identified; safe placement arranged if at immediate risk
Counselling referral arranged for elder; family conversation if appropriate (only if perpetrator not in household)
Weekly home visits (or settlement neutral location); safety plan reviewed weekly; de-escalation requires supervisor validation
Re-assess Q16, Q17; case may move from active crisis to monitored if stable for 8 weeks

content: Elder safe; safety plan documented and operational; no further safety incidents for 8 consecutive weeks before de-escalation.

articles.title: Domain 6 Pathway — Food & Financial Security

head: Phase
Action
Owner
rows: Food triage: which meal is missing? Who cooks? Why is access disrupted? Dry ration kit if emergency
Community kitchen enrolment if home-bound or family-cooking unreliable; centre attendance if mobile
Q18a addressed if relevant: soft food adaptation; dental referral; supplement if available
Re-assess Q18a, Q18b, Q19; review whether kitchen meal is being received and adequate
Entitlements team status check on referred items (ration card, pension); CO documents resolution or escalation
Full re-EVA on food and financial security; kitchen attendance reviewed; family financial situation if changed

content: Q18a and Q18b at 0 or 1; daily food access secured through one of: family / community kitchen / centre.

articles.title: Domain 7 Pathway — Emergency & Social Support

head: Phase
Action
Owner
rows: Identify one trusted contact in settlement or family; phone number recorded; introductions made
Emergency response plan: who is called first, second, third in case of fall/illness/distress; written and explained
Community kitchen and/or centre attendance to build daily contact; settlement leader briefed
Test the emergency contact chain (mock or actual minor event); refine if gaps
Re-assess Q21; if trusted contact not working, identify alternative
Full re-EVA; emergency response chain refreshed

content: Q21 at 0 or 1; elder has at least one reliable contact within 5 minutes' walk; CO contact known to elder and family.

articles.title: High Need — Template care plan
content: High Need elders have multiple vulnerabilities but are usually mobile or partially mobile.

ul: **Fortnightly** home visit by CO (full visit ~30 min)
Weekly community kitchen contact (delivery or in-person)
Monthly centre visit encouraged if mobility permits
Caregiver identified and supported (if present); caregiver burden assessed monthly
PHC/UPHC enrolment + regular follow-up; medication adherence monitored
Mobility aid procured if needed; home hazards addressed
Community kitchen if food access affected; centre attendance if mobile
Entitlements team referral active for any missing benefit

content: Full EVRAT quarterly. Event-triggered within 7 days of any deterioration event. Move to Moderate possible after 12 weeks of stable improvement.

articles.title: Moderate Need — Template care plan
content: Moderate Need elders have identifiable vulnerabilities but are managing day-to-day. The plan focuses on closing specific gaps and preventing deterioration.

ul: **Monthly** home visit by CO (full visit ~25 min)
Centre attendance recommended (twice weekly minimum) if mobile
Community kitchen if food access requires it
PHC linkage if NCDs present; medication routine confirmed
Centre engagement for social connection and meal
Entitlements referrals on any missing benefit

content: Full EVRAT quarterly. Event-triggered immediate. Move to Low Need after 6 months of sustained improvement.

articles.title: Low Need — Template care plan
content: Low Need elders are largely managing well with minor gaps. The plan keeps a light touch but maintains relationship.

ul: Identification of any unaddressed entitlement gap
Encouragement to participate in centre activities
Preventive health awareness (NCDs, falls, mental health)

content: Full EVRAT twice yearly. Event-triggered escalation. Move to Stable possible after 12 months of stability.

articles.title: Stable & Self-Managing — Template care plan
content: Stable elders are functioning well. The programme's role is light-touch monitoring, preventive engagement, and peer-mentor enrolment for those willing.

ul: **3-monthly** home visit or centre contact (~15 min)
Centre engagement encouraged; potential peer-mentor role for other elders
Light wellness check; IVS only (unless trigger detected)
Peer-mentor enrolment if willing — pairs with 2–3 Tier 2 or 3 elders

content: Full EVRAT annually unless event triggers earlier. Status reviewed every 3 months — Stable cannot remain unreviewed beyond 3 months.

content.quote: Stable elders are an asset to the programme. Their lived experience, mobility, and time make them ideal peer mentors. A flourishing peer-mentor cohort reduces CO load significantly and dignifies the Stable cohort with purpose.

articles.title: Framework — Sensitive fields & restricted access
content: Sensitive fields are visible to the CO conducting the assessment and to the programme manager / city lead. They are **not** visible on:

ul: Peer dashboards or shared MIS views accessible to non-elderly-team staff
**Q6b** — Caregiver burden (caregiver's voice; must not be shown to elder or other family)
**Q16** — Abuse or neglect (could put elder at greater risk if disclosed)
Any Section E observations naming violence, financial exploitation, or family conflict

content: If the instrument is paper-based, sensitive-marked pages are kept in the locked office file; not discussed with neighbours, other family members, or other COs except the supervisor. If digital, sensitive fields are encrypted and access-controlled per the MIS specification.

articles.title: Framework — Calibration & inter-rater reliability
content: EVRAT relies on assessor judgement for many items. Two assessors observing the same elder may produce different scores. The calibration protocol reduces variation before rollout and detects drift during operation.

content.text: Pre-rollout calibration (once, before any CO uses EVRAT)

ul: Identify 5 elders across the vulnerability spectrum (1 likely Stable, 2 likely Moderate, 1 likely High, 1 likely Profound)
Three COs and one supervisor assess each elder independently, on the same day, without consulting each other
Scores compared item by item. Items where COs disagree by ≥2 points are flagged for discussion
Refine the operational definition in the guidelines if needed, or refine training if the definition is fine but applied inconsistently
Repeat with a fresh set of 5 elders until **agreement on ≥80% of items** is achieved
Each quarter, two COs jointly visit 2 elders together — one in the calmer middle of the day, after the regular assessment is done
Each CO completes the EVRAT independently from observation
Scores compared at the next team meeting. Disagreements ≥2 points discussed and definitions refined
Results documented; major drift triggers a refresher training

content.quote: Calibration is a tool-improvement exercise, not a performance evaluation of COs. Disagreement is expected and useful — it reveals where the tool is weak, not where the CO is wrong.

articles.title: Framework — Living arrangement (contextual modifier)
content: Living arrangement is captured as a **baseline contextual field** (in Section A), not a scored item. It modifies how other findings are interpreted: a high dependency score in an elder living alone requires a different response from the same score in a household with adult children.

Living arrangement was free text in v3 (Section B only, Stable elders only). Vulnerable elders going through EVA had no living arrangement field at all. v4 captures it for everyone as a structured baseline field.

content.text: Event triggers (immediate reassessment regardless of cadence)

articles.title: Framework — Supervisor validation & downgrade protection
content: Downgrade from severe categories (High or Profound Need) requires:

This prevents underestimation of severe vulnerability and protects against premature de-escalation. Sustained 8 weeks of stability minimum before considering Profound → High; 12 weeks before High → Moderate; 6 months before Moderate → Low.

ul: Review the previous Section E2 tasks: how many are closed? How many are stuck?
Co-visit the elder with the CO before signing off the downgrade
Document the reason on the case file
Set the next reassessment cadence per the new category

articles.title: Framework — Dynamic reassessment philosophy
content: The assessment framework is dynamic and editable. Categories may change over time based on:
Any authorised staff member with access to elder records may: trigger reassessment, raise concern alerts, recommend escalation, request supervisor review.
Stable & Self-Managing status **should not remain unreviewed beyond 3 months**.
